tools.description: Close a GitHub issue with a closing comment. Use this when work is complete, the issue is no longer relevant, or it's a duplicate. The closing comment should explain the resolution or reason for closing. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be closed. Target: *.

body.description: Closing comment explaining why the issue is being closed and summarizing any resolution, workaround, or conclusion.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to close. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 901 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/901). If omitted, closes the issue that triggered this workflow (requires an issue event trigger).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

state_reason.description: The reason for closing the issue. Use 'COMPLETED' for resolved issues, 'NOT_PLANNED' for issues that won't be addressed, or 'DUPLICATE' for duplicate issues. Defaults to 'COMPLETED'.

tools.name: close_issue

tools.description: Add a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. Target: *.

body.description: The comment text in Markdown format. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for this comment. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Auto-generated if not provided. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.

tools.name: add_comment

tools.description: Add labels to an existing GitHub issue or pull request for categorization and filtering. Labels must already exist in the repository. For creating new issues with labels, use create_issue with the labels property instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be added. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to add labels to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, adds labels to the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to add (e.g., ['bug', 'priority-high']). Labels must exist in the repository. This field is required — omitting it will cause a validation error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: add_labels

tools.description: Remove labels from an existing GitHub issue or pull request. If a label is not present on the item, it will be silently skipped. Use this to clean up labels that are no longer applicable. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be removed. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to remove labels from. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, removes labels from the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to remove (e.g., ['bug', 'needs-triage']). Labels that don't exist on the item are silently skipped. At least one label must be provided.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: remove_labels

tools.description: Assign one or more GitHub users to an issue. Use this to delegate work to specific team members. Users must have access to the repository.

assignee.description: Single GitHub username to assign. Use 'assignees' array for multiple users.

assignees.description: GitHub usernames to assign to the issue (e.g., ['octocat', 'mona']). Users must have access to the repository.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to assign users to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 543 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/543). If omitted, assigns to the issue that triggered this workflow.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: assign_to_user

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Post a routing comment @mentioning team owners on the triggering issue; bypasses safe-outputs mention neutralization

message.description: The comment body text without any @mentions or @ symbols

owners.description: Comma-separated GitHub usernames to notify, without the @ prefix (e.g. 'user1, user2, Azure/team-name')

tools.name: mention_owners

annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_issues

content.text: {"number":58008,"title":"[Identity] Docs: Update README to reflect TypeForwarding facade architecture in 1.53.0","body":"## Documentation Gap **Package:** `Azure.Identity` **Service directory:** `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` **Triggered by:** PR #57200 by `@m-nash` ## What Changed PR #57200 moved all `Azure.Identity` public types into `Azure.Core` and converted `Azure.Identity` into a lightweight `TypeForwardedTo` facade. A `MigrationGuide.md` was added to `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` covering CS0433 upgrade scenarios. CHANGELOG entries were added but the main **README.md was not updated** to reflect this architectural change. ## Gaps Found - The `README.md` still describes `Azure.Identity` as the home of the credential implementations, with no mention that types now live in `Azure.Core` and are type-forwarded - No link to `MigrationGuide.md` anywhere in the README (e.g. under "Getting started", "Next steps", or a dedicated "Upgrading" section) - The Azure.Core `README.md` key-concepts section lists `TokenCredentials` as an abstraction but never mentions that `DefaultAzureCredential` and all other credential types are now bundled in `Azure.Core` itself — users who read only the Azure.Core README have no way to discover this - No note in the `Azure.Identity` README that consumers of `Azure.Core` 1.53+ no longer need a separate `Azure.Identity` package reference to use credentials 📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files #### File 1: `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` — **edit** Add a callout near the top of the README (after the header links, before or at the start of **Getting started**) to inform users about the TypeForwarding change and point them to the migration guide: ```markdown > **Note (v1.53.0+):** Starting with version 1.53.0, all `Azure.Identity` types have been consolidated into `Azure.Core` and are forwarded from this package using [`TypeForwardedTo`]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted) Existing code continues to compile and run without changes. If you encounter [CS0433]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted) errors after upgrading, see the [Migration Guide](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md). ``` Additionally, add a link in the **Next steps** section: ```markdown - [Migration guide for Azure.Identity type consolidation into Azure.Core](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md) ``` #### File 2: `sdk/core/Azure.Core/README.md` — **edit** In the **Key concepts** section, update the bullet describing credential abstractions to note that credential implementations are now bundled in Azure.Core itself: Replace: ```markdown - Abstractions for representing Azure SDK credentials. (`TokenCredentials`). ``` With: ```markdown - Abstractions and implementations for authenticating Azure SDK clients (`TokenCredential`, `DefaultAzureCredential`, and all other credential types from `Azure.Identity` are now included in `Azure.Core` starting with version 1.53.0 — no separate `Azure.Identity` package reference is needed). ``` Also add a note to the **Getting started** section: ```markdown ### Authentication Starting with `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all authentication credential types (such as `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`, etc.) are bundled directly in this package. A separate `Azure.Identity` reference is no longer required. If you are upgrading and encounter CS0433 errors, see the [Azure.Identity migration guide](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md). ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No new snippet test files are required for these prose-only README additions. The existing credential usage snippets (e.g., `DefaultAzureCredential` in `sdk/core/Azure.Core/tests/`) already demonstrate credential usage. ### Step 3: Verify README structure Both READMEs already contain the required sections (Getting started, Key concepts, Examples, Troubleshooting, Next steps, Contributing). No structural additions are needed — only content updates within existing sections. ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order: 1. `dotnet build sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 identity` 4. `eng/scripts/Export-API.ps1 identity` 5. `dotnet format sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` And for Azure.Core: 1. `dotnet build sdk/core/Azure.Core/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/core/Azure.Core/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 core` ## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above > Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24202891168/agentic_workflow) · ● 612.2K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22&type=issues) ","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/58008","user":{"login":"github-actions[bot]","id":41898282,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/github-actions","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4"},"author_association":"NONE","reactions":{"total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"created_at":"2026-04-09T17:10:29Z","updated_at":"2026-04-09T17:10:29Z"}

{"number":58008,"title":"[Identity] Docs: Update README to reflect TypeForwarding facade architecture in 1.53.0","body":"## Documentation Gap **Package:** `Azure.Identity` **Service directory:** `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` **Triggered by:** PR #57200 by `@m-nash` ## What Changed PR #57200 moved all `Azure.Identity` public types into `Azure.Core` and converted `Azure.Identity` into a lightweight `TypeForwardedTo` facade. A `MigrationGuide.md` was added to `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` covering CS0433 upgrade scenarios. CHANGELOG entries were added but the main **README.md was not updated** to reflect this architectural change. ## Gaps Found - The `README.md` still describes `Azure.Identity` as the home of the credential implementations, with no mention that types now live in `Azure.Core` and are type-forwarded - No link to `MigrationGuide.md` anywhere in the README (e.g. under "Getting started", "Next steps", or a dedicated "Upgrading" section) - The Azure.Core `README.md` key-concepts section lists `TokenCredentials` as an abstraction but never mentions that `DefaultAzureCredential` and all other credential types are now bundled in `Azure.Core` itself — users who read only the Azure.Core README have no way to discover this - No note in the `Azure.Identity` README that consumers of `Azure.Core` 1.53+ no longer need a separate `Azure.Identity` package reference to use credentials 📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files #### File 1: `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` — **edit** Add a callout near the top of the README (after the header links, before or at the start of **Getting started**) to inform users about the TypeForwarding change and point them to the migration guide: ```markdown > **Note (v1.53.0+):** Starting with version 1.53.0, all `Azure.Identity` types have been consolidated into `Azure.Core` and are forwarded from this package using [`TypeForwardedTo`]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted) Existing code continues to compile and run without changes. If you encounter [CS0433]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted) errors after upgrading, see the [Migration Guide](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md). ``` Additionally, add a link in the **Next steps** section: ```markdown - [Migration guide for Azure.Identity type consolidation into Azure.Core](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md) ``` #### File 2: `sdk/core/Azure.Core/README.md` — **edit** In the **Key concepts** section, update the bullet describing credential abstractions to note that credential implementations are now bundled in Azure.Core itself: Replace: ```markdown - Abstractions for representing Azure SDK credentials. (`TokenCredentials`). ``` With: ```markdown - Abstractions and implementations for authenticating Azure SDK clients (`TokenCredential`, `DefaultAzureCredential`, and all other credential types from `Azure.Identity` are now included in `Azure.Core` starting with version 1.53.0 — no separate `Azure.Identity` package reference is needed). ``` Also add a note to the **Getting started** section: ```markdown ### Authentication Starting with `Azure.Core` 1.53.0, all authentication credential types (such as `DefaultAzureCredential`, `ClientSecretCredential`, `ManagedIdentityCredential`, etc.) are bundled directly in this package. A separate `Azure.Identity` reference is no longer required. If you are upgrading and encounter CS0433 errors, see the [Azure.Identity migration guide](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md). ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No new snippet test files are required for these prose-only README additions. The existing credential usage snippets (e.g., `DefaultAzureCredential` in `sdk/core/Azure.Core/tests/`) already demonstrate credential usage. ### Step 3: Verify README structure Both READMEs already contain the required sections (Getting started, Key concepts, Examples, Troubleshooting, Next steps, Contributing). No structural additions are needed — only content updates within existing sections. ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order: 1. `dotnet build sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 identity` 4. `eng/scripts/Export-API.ps1 identity` 5. `dotnet format sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/` And for Azure.Core: 1. `dotnet build sdk/core/Azure.Core/` 2. `dotnet test sdk/core/Azure.Core/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` 3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 core` ## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above > Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24202891168/agentic_workflow) · ● 612.2K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22&type=issues) ","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/58008","user":{"login":"github-actions[bot]","id":41898282,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/github-actions","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4"},"author_association":"NONE","reactions":{"total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"created_at":"2026-04-09T17:10:29Z","updated_at":"2026-04-09T17:10:29Z"}

{"total_count":147,"incomplete_results":false,"items":[{"id":3997335101,"number":56525,"state":"closed","state_reason":"completed","locked":false,"title":"[QUERY] "Configuration and Dependency Injection" link on latest `Azure.Identity` release (1.18.0) is broken","body":"### Library name and version

Azure.Identity 1.18.0

### Query/Question

Just received the release notification for [`Azure.Identity 1.18.0`](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/releases/tag/Azure.Identity_1.18.0) and immediately saw this section:

> Added experimental Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration and Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection integration for Azure SDK clients. For details, see the [Configuration and Dependency Injection](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/release/Azure.Identity_1.18.0/sdk/core/Azure.Core/src/docs/ConfigurationAndDependencyInjection.md) documentation.

But this link is broken.

I'm extremely interested in what this new capability is so would really appreciate if someone could fix or point to the correct document.

### Environment

This repository is just too broad. Can you consider splitting it?","body":"### Library name and version

N/A

### Query/Question

I have the habit of tracking release updates for every dependency we use in the company, which I usually do through the release subscription on the repository itself when that is available.

This particular repository however produces a TON of completely unrelated packages, making it much harder to track things we actually use: every time I have release notifications from here, it`s a dozen packages I'm not interested in. Rarely, one that we _actually_ consume, such as `Azure.Identity`, shows up and then I have to always filter out the noise to find those.

Could the team reconsider the repository organization here to split the different areas of interest into different repositories that people can track separately?

### Environment

NullReferenceException in DefaultAzureCredentialFactory","body":"### Library name and version

Azure.Identity 1.15.0

### Describe the bug

When I run my project in Visual Studio I'm getting NullReferenceException in this line:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/[REDACTED]/sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/src/DefaultAzureCredentialFactory.cs#L326

This doesn't happen when I run the project from command line with `dotnet run`

Adding these two options fixes the exception:
```
        ExcludeBrokerCredential = true,
        ExcludeVisualStudioCodeCredential = true,
```

### Expected behavior

No exception, this works when Azure.Identity is downgraded to 1.14.2

### Actual behavior

> Exception thrown: 'System.NullReferenceException' in Azure.Identity.dll

### Reproduction Steps

I can reproduce it in my project just by upgrading Azure.Identity from 1.14.2 to 1.15.0

### Environment

Latest version of Visual Studio on Windows No serverless connection error on AIProjectClient.GetChatCompletionsClient","body":"### Library name and version

Azure.AI.Projects v1.0.0-beta.5

### Describe the bug

projectClient.GetChatCompletionsClient() results in the following error:

`No connections found for 'Serverless'. At least one connection is required. Please add a new connection in the Azure AI Foundry portal by following the instructions here: https://aka.ms/azsdk/azure-ai-projects/how-to/connections-add`

The error does not occur when using 1.0.0-beta.3.


### Expected behavior

Connection is found without error

### Actual behavior

Error:

`No connections found for 'Serverless'. At least one connection is required. Please add a new connection in the Azure AI Foundry portal by following the instructions here: https://aka.ms/azsdk/azure-ai-projects/how-to/connections-add`

### Reproduction Steps

Follow instructions at https://microsoftlearning.github.io/mslearn-ai-studio/Instructions/02a-AI-foundry-sdk.html but use

```
dotnet add package Azure.Identity
dotnet add package Azure.AI.Projects --prerelease
dotnet add package Azure.AI.Inference --prerelease
```

instead of

```
dotnet add package Azure.Identity
dotnet add package Azure.AI.Projects --version 1.0.0-beta.3
dotnet add package Azure.AI.Inference --version 1.0.0-beta.3
```

### Environment

App Service Auth Fails with 500 When Using Groups and Managed Identity","body":"### Library name and version

Azure.Identity

### Describe the bug

When adding an Identity provider to an Azure App Service, web app or function app, we would like to specify "Identity requirements" using both 'allowedPrincipals.identities' and 'allowedPrincipals.groups'. The app registration is setup to include groups memberships in the token. This is working as expected when using an Entra Id user account.

We would like to access the protected resource from another app service, using its system assigned managed identity. This works as expected as long as we are not specifying 'allowedPrincipals.groups' on the identity provider for the protected app service. The JWT for the system assigned managed identity will not include the group claims and therefor the protected app service will crash returning '500 InternalServerError' before even invoking the application code. As soon as the 'allowedPrincipals.groups' is removed/cleared, everything works as expected and the application code will be invoked.


### The identity provider for auth-api:
```
"identityProviders": {
  "azureActiveDirectory": {
	"enabled": true,
	"registration": {
	  "openIdIssuer": "https://sts.windows.net/xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx/v2.0",
	  "clientId": "[app-reg-guid]"
	  "clientSecretSettingName": "MICROSOFT_PROVIDER_AUTHENTICATION_SECRET"
	},
	"login": {
	  "disableWWWAuthenticate": false
	},
	"validation": {
	  "jwtClaimChecks": {},
	  "allowedAudiences": [],
	  "defaultAuthorizationPolicy": {
		"allowedPrincipals": {
		  "identities": [
			"[consumer-of-atuh-api-managed-id-guid]"
		  ]
		}
	  }
	}
  }
```

### The identity provider for groups-auth-api:
```
"identityProviders": {
  "azureActiveDirectory": {
	"enabled": true,
	"registration": {
	  "openIdIssuer": "https://sts.windows.net/xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx/v2.0",
	  "clientId": "[app-reg-guid]"
	  "clientSecretSettingName": "MICROSOFT_PROVIDER_AUTHENTICATION_SECRET"
	},
	"login": {
	  "disableWWWAuthenticate": false
	},
	"validation": {
	  "jwtClaimChecks": {},
	  "allowedAudiences": [],
	  "defaultAuthorizationPolicy": {
		"allowedPrincipals": {
		  "groups": [
			"[some-group-object-id]"
		  ],
		  "identities": [
			"[consumer-of-atuh-api-managed-id-guid]"
		  ]
		}
	  }
	}
  }
```


### Expected behavior

If a system-assigned managed identity is listed under allowedPrincipals.identities, access should be granted even if allowedPrincipals.groups is also specified.

### Actual behavior

Calling the groups-auth-api will return a '500 InternalServerError'

### Reproduction Steps

	Resources:
		- 'allowedPrincipals.identities' protected: 
			auth-api
		- 'allowedPrincipals.identities' and 'allowedPrincipals.groups' protected 
			groups-auth-api

		- api consumer: 
			consumer-of-auth-api

The api consumer has a system assigned managed identity and will request a JWT token to be used as a [REDACTED] when calling both the apis.
- Calling the auth-api will return a '200 OK' response with json data in the response body.
- Calling the groups-auth-api will return a '500 InternalServerError'



### Environment

arguments.body: Thank you for your feedback. Tagging and routing to the team member(s) best able to assist.

## 🎯 Agentic Issue Triage

**Summary:** This bot-filed issue requests README documentation updates for `Azure.Identity` to reflect the TypeForwarding architecture change introduced in v1.53.0, where all credential types were consolidated into `Azure.Core`.

<details>
<summary>📋 Issue Details</summary>

**Package:** `Azure.Identity` (v1.53.0+)
**Affected API:** `README.md` for `Azure.Identity` and `Azure.Core`
**Scenarios:**
- `Azure.Identity` README does not mention that types now live in `Azure.Core` and are type-forwarded
- No link to `MigrationGuide.md` in the `Azure.Identity` README
- `Azure.Core` README key concepts section does not mention that credential types are now bundled in `Azure.Core` 1.53+
- No note that consumers of `Azure.Core` 1.53+ no longer need a separate `Azure.Identity` package reference

**Root ask:** Update `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` and `sdk/core/Azure.Core/README.md` to reflect the TypeForwarding facade architecture change and link to the existing `MigrationGuide.md`.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🔎 Debugging / Reproduction Notes</summary>

This is a documentation-only issue filed by automation (github-actions[bot]). No code behavior is broken. The issue was triggered by PR #57200 which moved `Azure.Identity` public types into `Azure.Core` using `TypeForwardedTo`, creating a gap between the architectural change and the published README content.

**Suggested investigation steps:**
1. Review PR #57200 to understand the full scope of the TypeForwarding change
2. Review `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/MigrationGuide.md` for the migration content that should be linked
3. Update `sdk/identity/Azure.Identity/README.md` to add a callout near the top about the TypeForwarding change (v1.53.0+) and a link to the migration guide
4. Update `sdk/core/Azure.Core/README.md` Key Concepts section to mention that credential types are now bundled in Azure.Core 1.53+
</details>

<details>
<summary>🏷️ Label Confidence</summary>

- **Category:** `Client` — `Azure.Identity` is a non-management client library (color #ffeb77 ✓); confirmed by quality reference issues #56525 and #52122
- **Service:** `Azure.Identity` — the issue explicitly names the `Azure.Identity` package (color #e99695 ✓); confirmed by same reference issues
- **Confidence:** High — issue explicitly names the package, no ambiguity between services or categories; matches patterns from quality reference issues
</details>

<details>
<summary>👥 Owner Routing</summary>

- **Matched CODEOWNERS entry:** `# ServiceLabel: %Azure.Identity` (line 69) — the service label "Azure.Identity" matches this entry
- **AzureSdkOwners:** @JonathanCrd
- **ServiceOwners:** none listed
- **Routing action:** Assigned `@JonathanCrd`; "needs-team-attention" not added (issue is not customer-reported — filed by bot)
- **Scan notes:** Scanned from the bottom of CODEOWNERS upward. No more-specific multi-label entries involving "Azure.Identity" were encountered. Line 69 was the first and only matching entry.
</details>
